item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This reinforces my belief that an SCP article can be a work of art. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Are you thinking of SCP-3007?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really liked this, kinda like a multiverse-foundation. But I did notice that the artwork at the bottom had a similar art style to another article… I can't remember which one except that it depicted one world ending… and what caused it spreading.

Also, What happens when the archivist's universe runs out of energy?

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It gave me some Hitchhiker's guide vibes
+1

item.title: <I am disinterested in other universes, for they hold no turtles.>
item.content:encoded: This is so goddamn good, I love the concept of this alternate dimension deity-ish Foundation that collects things. I absolutely love it, and the way it's written, especially with the twist at the end, it's fantastic. The idea that even in the end, the Archivists continue and are unchanging, is so enchanting and bizarrely emotional. Found my new favorite scip, thanks. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Not my cup of tea, but I did enjoy the writing style.

Also, this feels like if Douglas Adams wrote Homestuck.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This formatting is EPIC!!!!!!!!!!!!!!

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'll revisit this page every once in a while and reread, and my enthusiasm for it has only grown. This is absolutely one of my favorite works on the wiki, and one of my favorite SCPs. It looks beautiful on an aesthetic level, and the formatting is exceptional. It's also a well-executed format screw. The anomaly itself is also captivating, and the story is one that appeals to me.

I love this SCP, and I really hope that the author writes more.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't quite understand why the foundation could possibly obtain the last few documents, if I got what you meant.

it wasn't the Foundation that obtained the last few documents from the Archivists. more the other way around ;)

Also I wonder if this is inspired by the book The Three Body Problem?

noop. but I've heard a lot of rave reviews of so I think I'll be giving it a read :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
One of my favorites. It’s massively aesthetically pleasing, the concept is fun, the execution is good, and the ending fills me with an emotion that doesn’t exist.

+1000

item.title: If you liked this..
item.content:encoded: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=C6eOcd06kdk

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
…The Archivists are cute…

Also, I super love making a conlang to talk to them! That's so great!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Even ignoring how good it is, this skip's name would make a perfect name for a Culture GOU.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't quite understand why the foundation could possibly obtain the last few documents, if I got what you meant.
Also I wonder if this is inspired by the book The Three Body Problem?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I found this one curiously uplifting. The idea that something of the universe survives beyond the utter end is rather pleasant.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like, but

6,908,017,318,855,191,004,119,2482,194,872,949

an extra digit snuck in there. (Unless the number sequence is somehow important, in which case the commas are off.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This page looks so good…

As for the SCP, it did a good job of standing in between the lines of technobabble and understandable-ness. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
These things actually remind of the Luminaries from the Pleroma.

The Pleroma is like a perfect dimension during which everything is perfect, that is, has been existing, has existed, and will always exist. Luminaries are like angels but not actually. They inhabit the Pleroma.

Below (or outside) the Pleroma is the Kenoma, which is this world, where everything is constantly degrading into nothingness. Pleroma is fullness, Kenoma is emptyness.

These beings adds the idea that the Aeons and the Luminaries are actually trying to save the Kenomic worlds from total non-existence, eventhough they consider these worlds as an abortion. Anyway, +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Flashily concise. Love it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
this is quite good

is there a definitive answer to the question "who popped up for 116 years?"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I thought the scp was thaumiel not because the Foundation is getting anything in return but just because the scp is preserving the universe.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Dear god I love the "SUMMARY OF UNIVERSE #139331920100933

It was mostly nothing.

But there were some interesting parts."

I think I want that on my grave.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'd like to think the entropy reducers were the very last descendants of humanity having one last big party as the black hole they gathered around reached a critically low point and exploded. That would have been one hell of a party.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I can't help but think of Discworld, given the Archivists' preoccupation with turtles.

This is a really, really fun article. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Alright, so I lost the initial document describing it, so me and that friend recreated it.

https://docs.google.com/document/d/1rmMtPkGBwpkqen8CVNjhKRjXgdp8l9AW1g-EpWYOwyE/edit?usp=sharing

Admittedly, before this one came along and with some major tweaks to it, I could have probably turned it into an SCP. But oh well, your's is better than the final result of that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I tried changing it around a bit again, do you think it's any better now?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The new ending, while good and interesting, lacks the charm of the old one (that felt like a h2g2 reference).
Still +1 anyway, great stuff.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, this is certainly my jam. Sometimes, style is substance.

I got some serious Marathon (the game) vibes from this, both from the visual style (dithering!) and the mix of in- and out-of-"character" information. No idea if this was an influence or a coincidence, but the resemblance is of the best type (reminiscent but not derivative).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is really good, especially the cognitohazard. I had to look at the comments to get the final twist, but once I got it it all made sense. I mean, if extradimensional ball-lightning aliens want to make cool pixel fanart of the Foundation and include it in the document, they're totally allowed to make cool pixel fanart of the Foundation and include it in the document

+1

item.title: Re: yay!
item.content:encoded:
Kind of like a multiverse-dwelling version of the Foundation, except one that focuses purely on preservation of items, not world defense.

You nailed it!

In regards to the alien font, I would think that a hyperdimensional multiverse-traveling alien civilization would have a writing system that's, well, weirder. At the very least it would be most likely logographic, not alphabetic, because they don't communicate by speech and also based on how you translated their responses in the logs - as in, more like Chinese than English, the characters would be more complicated and numerous/diverse and you wouldn't see the same ones pop up multiple times in a few sentences like you see in the image, except maybe for a few common ones like «MULTITUDE-GROUP» / «loc. WITHIN» / «BE/EXIST» / «HAVE». In addition, perhaps have it written vertically or in an even 5x5 square to create ambiguity as to how it's supposed to be read? I could make something like this for you if you want

Damn, I tried something like this originally but I never had enough time or creativity or experience in conlanging. If you want to make something more inspired, I would absolutely love it!

item.title: Re: yay!
item.content:encoded: It's from Buck Godot, by Phil and Kaja Foglio (They currently do the Girl Genius webcomic). It also appears in various other works by them.

item.title: Re: yay!
item.content:encoded:
Great article, I love it :) Kind of like a multiverse-dwelling version of the Foundation, except one that focuses purely on preservation of items, not world defense. I wonder whether there are any living beings that use, enjoy, or interact with the items in the archive, or if they are just preserved as an end in itself.

In regards to the alien font, I would think that a hyperdimensional multiverse-traveling alien civilization would have a writing system that's, well, weirder. At the very least it would be most likely logographic, not alphabetic, because they don't communicate by speech and also based on how you translated their responses in the logs - as in, more like Chinese than English, the characters would be more complicated and numerous/diverse and you wouldn't see the same ones pop up multiple times in a few sentences like you see in the image, except maybe for a few common ones like «MULTITUDE-GROUP» / «loc. WITHIN» / «BE/EXIST» / «HAVE». In addition, perhaps have it written vertically or in an even 5x5 square to create ambiguity as to how it's supposed to be read? I could make something like this for you if you want

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ah, those are just extras I had while working that I hid to keep everything clean. They're redundant now, but I think I'll leave them as extras for people to find.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The source code still has some Imgur links to some images along with some hidden lines. Are you intending to show them or keep them hidden?

item.title: Re: yay!
item.content:encoded: That's from HHGTTG, right?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As some people have speculated here, the Foundation is actively trading and making agreements with the Archivists, who are basically an alien species (ish). I think the security is necessary.

I think that it can be assumed that the Foundation considers security to be necessary everywhere. Putting all the killer glyph stuff is becoming a weird stylistic thing that probably bugs me more than it should. I mean, I have used it too, (although I think that both times I did it was for a meaningful reason and in one case is structurallyl essential to the piece) so I am not against it in principle, but I'd like to see it used only when it adds something contextual or to pacing or something, I am not sure that is the case here. Otherwise it can be come an overused trope.

There's also my usual issue with ListUsers: I just don't imagine "sirpudding" as a sensible in-universe username for a professional outfit like the Foundation.

Note though this isn't really a significant problem for me here and it really is the last image that I don't quite get.

Yup, it was a bit of a risky decision on my part. Overall, my intention with this was to create something that was somewhere between an SCP and a tale. The last two documents are intended to break immersion. The big white space between the last part of the documentation and the beginning of the Archivists' summary is what I used to signify a complete break in the traditional formatting of an SCP document.

I get that. What I don't get is what the archivists would make this still life of out-of-scale grainy green stuff for. It looks like an illustration.

It is also a bunch of very specific stuff that isn't representative of the universe (all baryonic matter, all from Earth, mostly anthropogenic, mostly 20th century).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
the last image totally breaks immersion for me (who put that there and who is the intended audience?)

I headcanoned this as signifying that the Archivists collected their own documentation. So the entire article - including the kill-meme, is something they have on-file themselves. The SCPF's version would be this, minus the bit from the skip's perspective.

Kinda like how SCP-2254 is SCPF documentation in the hands of Serpent's Hand members.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks bruh

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you! :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
For the love of god. Don't use bright yellow on white though. Or at least shadow-box the text. My eyes ;_;

Wait shit how do I do that

pls help

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you! :D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this, I think, but I don't know if the security theater stuff at the beginning is really justified or just cruft

As some people have speculated here, the Foundation is actively trading and making agreements with the Archivists, who are basically an alien species (ish). I think the security is necessary.

the last image totally breaks immersion for me (who put that there and who is the intended audience?)

Yup, it was a bit of a risky decision on my part. Overall, my intention with this was to create something that was somewhere between an SCP and a tale. The last two documents are intended to break immersion. The big white space between the last part of the documentation and the beginning of the Archivists' summary is what I used to signify a complete break in the traditional formatting of an SCP document.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That's about where I am as well. The stuff about heat death also seems to have been dropped in. I understand why the author is using it, but in-universe, who left it here on this document? Why?

I think all the elements here are worth including, but they could be introduced much more cleanly. Even a [Documents Received from Unknown Source] collapsible or something (for the last images) would make me feel better.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this, I think, but I don't know if the security theater stuff at the beginning is really justified or just cruft and the last image totally breaks immersion for me (who put that there and who is the intended audience?). I am going to have to think about whether that is enough to turn an upvote into a no-vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yup, typo. It's corrected now

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Is it weird if my reaction is along the lines of "These are super-cute"? Because they are, and this is a whole heap of neat stuff. :D

Not weird, that's kinda what I was going for :)

There are only eight listed. :C

Whoops, changed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think the hook here is that we don't know what the Foundation are getting back from co-operating with the lighty-guys. Given the object class, level of security, the fact they are co-operating in the first place, The Foundation must be getting something very important in return.

Though if that is the author's intention, maybe just a little clue to what that is would be nice. Or maybe I've just missed it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'll agree that some of the format stuff here is more style than anything, but it's pretty excellently done and looks amazing, so I can't hold that against it. What really makes this work for me is that a concept I kept expecting to shift into horror really turned out to be benevolent and kind, even when juxtaposed against something very grim. That's something I fall for whenever it's done, even if it's becoming a trope itself.

It also lines up with a lot of themes and ideas I want to write about. Maybe you'll see a tale with these folks soon??

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Suddenly, I'm imagining the Foundation as a place where regularly you're looking around at stuff, when out of the blue, SCREAMER ON YOUR SCREEN! Then, "Life signs detected. You may proceed. Ding!"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was looking through the page sources and:

Metal doggos
Cube doggos
Sphere doggos

were names of the pictures.
+1 dude.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This captures my sentiment quite well.

This has decent pacing, but it is marred be a certain lack of imagination on the part of the beings themselves; balls of light don't provide much opportunity for interesting imagery. Instead, they feel like the most generic/safe route to take in this regard.

The last document going into detail about heat death seems a little out-of-the-blue - it doesn't really serve any purpose to the narrative. I mean, does it really matter how the universe ends? It's the fact that it's ending, not the details, that provide the impetus for the creatures to collect information.

I think this is symptomatic of the lack of a consistent voice. I'm not sure how I'm supposed to feel by the end of this; it moves from an exploration of light spheres engaged in the trade of oddly specific objects to an exposition on heat death, and while the link between action and motive makes logical sense, it doesn't resonate with me. It's probably because the mechanism behind how the light spheres actual archive things is brushed over. I have no idea how many of these things there actually are (or how many different universes there are), how difficult they perceive their tasks to be, and where this particular museum is. Delving into these kinds of details may have helped give this a stronger identity and staying power for me outside of the (admittedly good-quality) visual presentation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm torn on this. On the one hand, everything here reeks of high production values. The attention to detail is painfully beautiful; every one of the graphics is of immense quality and it feels like a huge amount of effort went into making this piece as cosmetically appealing as possible. It's exceptionally well-written, technically flawless, and the way it's presented succeeded in engaging me with flying colors.

On the other hand, I feel like the concept you went in with is finally executed in the most absolute boring way possible. Universes die. There's a group of smart beings that catalog materials from these universes. And that's the story.

I'm also not the biggest fan of the way the skip ends - the artwork is haunting and quite pretty, but it's the conclusion to an official document, which just doesn't sit right with me. You pay careful detail throughout the piece to article continuity and practical in-universe measures, but then right at the end it seems to just tell the format to go fuck itself. Nominally I'd be more than fine with that, only it's incredibly on the nose and lacks any subtlety whatsoever - literally painting the group's mission statement on a star-ridden wall.

I want to upvote this so very, very much - but the failure to expand or really do anything with an initially mildly interesting but somewhat insubstantial idea is what firmly pushes this into novote territory for me, with the caveat that I WANT TO SEE MORE OF THIS. Visually it's all incredibly well-done and if there were a story with just a bit more flair or intrigue I would be clamoring for supplements and documents and blaugh

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I personally found this story very simplistic, but engaging.

Which is perfect- that's exactly what I want most skips to be, because it allows them to be memorable.

+1 bruh

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Format screw is really cool. I love the actual, simulated cognitohazard.

Description contains this line:

By order, the first nine SCP-3201 instances expressed the desire to collect "ideal versions" of the following items:

…but there's only 8 listed. Is this a typo?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Extremely well written! At first I thought it seemed similar to SCP-702, but the format screw and the extra information brought it well beyond that level.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So, from a writing perspective, this is very weak, concept wise. What saves this is the execution, for sure.

There's a *lot* of formatting and hand-done graphics here, and for that, you easily get my upvote for effort, and skill alone (plus it was fun to read).

For the love of god. Don't use bright yellow on white though. Or at least shadow-box the text. My eyes ;_;

item.title: Re: yay!
item.content:encoded:
http://www.rochesterfantasyfans.org/goodies/winslow1.jpg
http://www.rochesterfantasyfans.org/goodies/winslow2.jpg
http://www.rochesterfantasyfans.org/goodies/winslow3.jpg

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Loved the article although I could only read part of it due to having a small amount of time. I love the format screws, mainly the images that change to make it look like a real computer.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Is it weird if my reaction is along the lines of "These are super-cute"? Because they are, and this is a whole heap of neat stuff. :D

EDIT: Oh yeah, this:

By order, the first nine SCP-3201 instances expressed the desire to collect "ideal versions" of the following items:

There are only eight listed. :C

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh wow, thank you!
